perturbation.original_text: University represents that informed consent was required from all individual subjects prior to enrollment in the Program, and that the Program was approved by the Institutional Review Board of the University.
perturbation.changed_text: University represents that the Program was approved by the Institutional Review Board of the University.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit representation about informed consent from all subjects creates a potential legal contradiction. While IRB approval is mentioned, the explicit guarantee of informed consent is omitted, potentially opening ArTara to liability if subjects' rights were violated during the original Program.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR Part 50 - Protection of Human Subjects
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 50.20 mandates that no investigator may involve a human being as a subject in research covered by these regulations unless the investigator has obtained the legally effective informed consent of the subject or the subject's legally authorized representative. The modified text removes University's explicit representation that informed consent was obtained from all subjects, contradicting the requirement that such consent *must* be obtained.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: s? The FDA regulations do not preclude a member from being compensated for services rendered. Payment to IRB members should not be related to or dependent upon a favorable decision. Expenses, such as travel costs, may also be reimbursed. 9. What is the FDA role in IRB liability in malpractice suits? FDA regulations do not address the question of IRB or institutional liability in the case of malpractice suits. FDA does not have authority to limit liability of IRBs or their members. Compliance with FDA regulations may help minimize an IRB's exposure to liability. 10. Is the purpose of the IRB review of informed consent to protect the institution or the subject? The fundamental purpose of IRB review of informed consent is to assure that the rights and welfare of subjects are protected. A signed informed consent document is evidence that the document has been provided to a prospective subject (and presumably, explained) and that the subject has agreed to participate in the research. IRB review of informed consent documents also ensures that the institution has complied with applicable regulations. 11. Does an IRB or institution have to compensate subjects if injury occurs as a result of participation in a research study? Institutional policy, not FDA regulation, determines whether compensation and medical treatment(s) will be offered and the conditions that might be placed on subject eligibility for compensation or treatment(s). The FDA informed consent regulation on compensation [21 CFR 50.25(a)(6)] requires that, for research involving more than minimal risk, the subject must be told whether any compensation and any medical treatment(s) are available if injury occurs and, if so, what they are, or where further information may be obtained. Any statement that compensation is not offered must avoid waiving or appearing to waive any of the subject's rights or releasing or appearing to release the investigator, sponsor, or institution from liability for negligence [21 CFR 50.20]. II. IRB Membership 12. May a clinical investigator be an IRB member? Yes, however, the IRB regulations [21 CFR 56.107(e)] prohibit any member from participating in the IRB's initial or continuing review of any study in which the member has a conflicting interest, except to provide information requested by the IRB. When selecting IRB members, the potential for conflicts of interest should be considered. When members frequently have conflicts and must absent themselves from deliberation and abstain from voting, their contributions to the group review process may be diminished and could hinder the review procedure. Even greater disruptions may result if this person is chairperson of the IRB. 13. The IRB regulations require an IRB to have a diverse membership. May one member satisfy more than one membership category? Yes. For example, one member could be otherwise unaffiliated with the institution and have a primary concern in a non-scientific area. This individual would satisfy two of the membership requirements of the regulations. IRBs should strive, however, for a membership that has a diversity of representative capacities and disciplines. 1 14. When IRB members cannot attend a convened meeting, may they send someone from their department to vote for them? No. Alternates who are formally appointed and listed in the membership roster may substitute, but ad hoc substitutes are not permissible as members of an IRB. However, a member who is unable to be present at the convened meeting may participate by video-conference or conference telephone call, when the member has received a copy of the documents that are to be reviewed at the meeting. Such members may vote and be counted as part of the quorum. If allowed by IRB procedures, ad hoc substitutes may attend as consultants and gather information for the absent member, but they may not be counted toward the quorum or participate in either deliberation or voting with the board. The IRB may, of course, ask questions of
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the explicit representation from the University that informed consent was obtained from all subjects. The Law Explanation states that 21 CFR § 50.20 mandates that no investigator may involve a human being as a subject in research unless informed consent has been obtained. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 21 CFR Part 50, which covers the protection of human subjects and includes general requirements for informed consent in § 50.20. By removing the explicit guarantee of informed consent, the changed text contradicts the legal requirement for such consent, as the explicit representation of informed consent from all subjects prior to enrollment in the Program is removed, leaving ArTara potentially liable. This falls under the Omission Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: University represents that to the best of its knowledge and belief there are no outstanding agreements or assignments which are inconsistent with the rights granted to ArTara pursuant to Article Four.
perturbation.changed_text: University represents that there are no *known* outstanding agreements or assignments which are inconsistent with the rights granted to ArTara pursuant to Article Four.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "to the best of its knowledge and belief" to "there are no *known*", the representation is weakened. A future claim could argue that the University *should* have known, even if they didn't. This creates a risk because Iowa law imposes a duty of due diligence in such situations.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Iowa Code § 622.1 relates to witness competency, the broader legal principle in Iowa is that parties entering contracts have a duty to exercise reasonable diligence in ascertaining relevant facts. By removing the explicit 'belief' and focusing solely on 'known' agreements, the contract creates a loophole where the University could claim ignorance even if a reasonable inquiry would have revealed conflicting agreements, potentially violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing inherent in Iowa contract law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract language, "to the best of its knowledge and belief," suggests a comprehensive representation by the University. The changed text, using "there are no *known*," weakens this representation by implying that the University only warrants against what it is explicitly aware of, regardless of whether it *should* have known. This creates a loophole, as the University could argue ignorance even if a reasonable inquiry would have revealed conflicting agreements. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not directly usable due to formatting issues, the Law Explanation highlights that under Iowa law, parties entering contracts have a duty to exercise reasonable diligence in ascertaining relevant facts. The modification in the contract contradicts this implied duty of good faith and fair dealing inherent in contract law, as it allows the University to potentially avoid liability by claiming ignorance, even if it failed to conduct reasonable due diligence. This constitutes an Omission Legal Contradiction because the change removes the explicit mention of "belief", a factor contributing to due diligence.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties shall commence performance of the Project promptly after the date of last signature of this Agreement and shall perform the Project in accordance with the current state of the laboratory research art and in accordance with applicable state and federal laws, including export laws, and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties shall commence performance of the Project after the date of last signature of this Agreement and shall perform the Project in accordance with applicable state and federal laws and regulations.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'promptly' and 'current state of the laboratory research art and including export laws' loosens the obligation for timely and state-of-the-art performance and implies export laws are not of critical importance. Since the University is undertaking research activities it has a duty to follow export controls.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 22 U.S. Code § 2778 - Control of arms exports and imports
perturbation.law_explanation: 22 U.S. Code § 2778 controls the export of defense articles and services. Research data and technology can be considered controlled items. By removing the explicit acknowledgement of export law compliance and the reference to performing in accordance with the 'current state of the laboratory research art,' the contract weakens the University's obligation to comply with stringent federal export controls regarding the transfer of technical data related to the Project, especially if the data could be used for military or dual-use applications.

Control of arms exports and imports (a) Presidential control of exports and imports of defense articles and services, guidance of policy, etc.; designation of United States Munitions List; issuance of export licenses; negotiations information (1) In furtherance of world peace and the security and foreign
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates adherence to 'export laws' and performance 'in accordance with the current state of the laboratory research art.' The changed text omits these stipulations, thereby weakening the university's obligation to comply with federal export controls as highlighted in the Law Explanation and the Perturbation Explanation. Although the snippets only contain the title of 22 U.S. Code § 2778 and a list of sections related to the Arms Export Control Act (AECA), the Law Explanation states that research data can be considered controlled items, making this omission a potential violation of export regulations. Removing 'export laws' directly contradicts the need for compliance. The changed text fails to explicitly acknowledge and ensure adherence to these regulations, creating a legal contradiction.
